title: Pasta e fagioli
subtitle: The Roman pasta and bean soup
excerpt: Borlotti beans cooked down with pancetta, tomatoes, and rosemary, finished with small pasta stirred into the thick, brothy soup. One of the great Italian one-pot meals. Hearty enough for dinner, ready in 45 minutes with tinned beans.
sourceNotes: Pasta e fagioli is one of the classic dishes of Roman and central Italian home cooking, documented at least since the Renaissance. This version uses tinned borlotti beans following the modern Italian home-cook approach.

recipe.makeAheadNotes: Make the bean soup base up to two days ahead. Cook and add pasta only when ready to serve ,  pasta absorbs the broth and swells if left to stand.

glossaryTerms.definition: An Italian variety of cranberry bean with a creamy texture and earthy, slightly nutty flavour. Available dried or tinned. When cooked, the cream and red mottled skin fades to a uniform beige-pink. They are the bean of choice for pasta e fagioli and many Italian soups.

content.text: Pasta e fagioli sits at the border between soup and stew: thick enough to eat with a fork, but still poured from a ladle. The

are partially crushed at the end to thicken the broth, and the pasta is cooked directly in the soup so it absorbs the flavour as it softens. Use ditalini, small tubetti, or any small pasta shape. Do not try to make this ahead with the pasta already added ,  the pasta swells and absorbs all the liquid by the next day.

Heat {{extra-virgin-olive-oil}} of olive oil in a large saucepan over a medium heat. Add the {{lardons}} of pancetta and cook for 4 minutes until lightly golden. Add the {{onion}} onion and {{celery}} celery and cook for 8 minutes until soft.
Add the {{garlic}} of garlic and {{rosemary}} of rosemary and cook for 1 minute. Add {{tinned-tomatoes}} of tinned tomatoes and simmer for 5 minutes, breaking up the tomatoes with a spoon.

Add the {{borlotti-beans}} of drained borlotti beans and pour in {{stock-chicken}} of hot stock. Simmer for 10 minutes. Remove the rosemary sprig. Use the back of a spoon or a potato masher to crush about a third of the beans into the broth to thicken it.
Bring the soup to a brisk simmer. Add {{pasta-dried}} of pasta directly to the pot. Cook according to packet instructions until al dente, stirring often as the pasta will stick.
Taste and season with salt and black pepper. Ladle into bowls and drizzle with olive oil. Finish with grated Parmesan if using.

items.symptom: Pasta has turned the soup into a solid mass
items.cause: The pasta was left to stand in the soup after cooking.
items.fix: Cook the pasta only when ready to eat. If the soup has set, add hot water or stock and reheat gently, stirring. The soup will loosen back into a ladle-able consistency.

items.cause: Not enough beans were crushed to thicken the broth.
items.fix: Crush more beans against the side of the pot with a spoon, or blend a ladleful of the soup separately and stir it back in.

items.cause: Small pasta cooks faster than most people expect in a thick soup.
items.fix: Start checking ditalini at 7 minutes. It should be slightly underdone when you take the pot off the heat as it continues cooking in the residual heat.

items.cause: Italian bean soups need generous olive oil and a good hit of salt.
items.fix: The drizzle of olive oil at the end is not decoration: it carries flavour and brings the soup together. Use the best olive oil you have. Season generously and finish with black pepper.

content.text: Pasta e fagioli is one of the oldest dishes in the Italian tradition, documented in cookbooks since the 15th century. Like ribollita, it was born of economy: beans and pasta were the cheapest ingredients available. In Rome, where it is most closely associated, it is known as pasta e fasòl. Every region has a version; the Roman version uses borlotti beans and is thick, almost porridge-like.
